stem: How many distinct permutations are there of a set of \([[n]]\) distinct objects (i.e., what is \( |S_{[[n]]}| \))?
solution: There are \([[n]]\) choices for where the first object goes, \([[n]]-1\) for the second, and so on, giving \( |S_{[[n]]}| = [[n]]! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to include a factor of \([[n]]\), leaving only \(([[n]]-1)!\).
This allows repeats (functions, not permutations), an unrelated count.
This only accounts for placing the first two objects.
This counts subsets, not full orderings.
This is off by one above the correct count.
This is off by one below the correct count.

stem: A permutation \(\sigma\) of a \([[n]]\)-element set decomposes into disjoint cycles \( \left(1\ \ 2\ \ \cdots\ \ [[p]]\right) \) and \( \left([[p1]]\ \ \cdots\ \ [[pq]]\right) \), with every other element fixed. Find the order of \(\sigma\) (the smallest positive integer \(k\) with \( \sigma^k = e \)).
solution: Disjoint cycles commute, and the order of a product of disjoint cycles is the lcm of their lengths: \( \operatorname{lcm}([[p]],[[q]]) = [[answer]] \). Fixed points contribute a cycle length of 1 and do not affect the order.
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the cycle lengths instead of taking the lcm.
This adds the cycle lengths instead of taking the lcm.
This is only the length of the first cycle, ignoring the second.
This is only the length of the second cycle, ignoring the first.
This is off by one above the correct order.
This is off by one below the correct order.

stem: A permutation \(\sigma\) of a \([[n]]\)-element set decomposes into three disjoint cycles: \( \left(1\ \ 2\ \ \cdots\ \ [[p]]\right) \), \( \left([[p1]]\ \ \cdots\ \ [[pq]]\right) \), and \( \left([[pq1]]\ \ \cdots\ \ [[pqr]]\right) \), with every other element fixed. Find the order of \(\sigma\).
solution: The order of a product of disjoint cycles is the lcm of their lengths: \( \operatorname{lcm}([[p]],[[q]],[[r]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the three cycle lengths instead of taking the lcm.
This adds the three cycle lengths instead of taking the lcm.
This omits the third cycle from the lcm.
This omits the first cycle from the lcm.
This is off by one above the correct order.
This is off by one below the correct order.

stem: Two meshed gears are marked with a reference tooth. The small gear returns its marked tooth to the top after every \([[p]]\) rotations of the shuffle mechanism, while the large gear returns its marked tooth to the top after every \([[q]]\) rotations, and each rotation permutes the mechanism's state. What is the smallest number of rotations after which BOTH marked teeth are simultaneously back at the top?
solution: This is exactly the order of the permutation formed by two independent cycles of lengths \([[p]]\) and \([[q]]\): \( \operatorname{lcm}([[p]],[[q]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the two periods instead of taking the lcm.
This adds the two periods instead of taking the lcm.
This is only the small gear's period, ignoring the large gear.
This is only the large gear's period, ignoring the small gear.
This is off by one above the correct number of rotations.
This is off by one below the correct number of rotations.

stem: How many EVEN permutations are there of a set of \([[n]]\) distinct objects (i.e., what is \( |A_{[[n]]}| \), the order of the alternating group)?
solution: Exactly half of the \([[n]]!\) permutations of \(S_{[[n]]}\) are even, so \( |A_{[[n]]}| = [[n]]!/2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |S_{[[n]]}| \) itself, forgetting that only half are even.
This uses \((n-1)!\), an unrelated factorial.
This divides by 4 instead of 2, halving the count twice.
This mistakes \(n\) itself for the alternating group's order.
This is off by one above the correct order.
This is off by one below the correct order.
